title: Algebra 1
blurb: Unit-by-unit practice for the Algebra 1 course — expressions to quadratics, every unit with its own problem collection.

units.title: Expressions & Operations
units.blurb: Variables, evaluating and simplifying, exponents, order of operations, and words-to-algebra.

units.title: Solving Linear Equations
units.blurb: One-step to both-sides equations, fractions and special cases, and rearranging formulas.

units.title: Linear Inequalities
units.blurb: Solving and graphing, the negative-flip rule, compound and/or, and absolute-value equations.

units.title: Introduction to Functions
units.blurb: The one-output rule, f(x) notation, domain and range, and reading real-world graphs.

units.title: Linear Functions & Slope
units.blurb: Slope as a rate, y = mx + b, point-slope and standard forms, parallel and perpendicular lines.

units.title: Systems of Equations
units.blurb: Graphing, substitution, elimination, applications, and the no-solution/infinite cases.

units.title: Polynomials & Factoring
units.blurb: Polynomial arithmetic, GCF, factoring trinomials and special patterns.

units.title: Quadratic Equations & Functions
units.blurb: Solving by factoring, roots and the discriminant, the vertex, and parabola graphs.

forms.title: Evaluating expressions
forms.skill: Powers before products; substitute carefully.

variants.statement: Evaluate $2a^2 - 6b$ when $a = 2$ and $b = 6$.
variants.explanation: Square first, multiply second: $2\cdot 2^2 = 8$, then subtract $6\cdot 6 = 36$: answer $-28$. The square belongs to $a$ alone, not to $2a$.

forms.skill: x^a·x^b = x^(a+b); division subtracts exponents.

variants.statement: Simplify: $\dfrac{x^{2} \cdot x^{3}}{x^{3}}$.
variants.explanation: Multiplying powers adds exponents ($2+3=5$); dividing subtracts ($5-3=2$). Never multiply the exponents.

forms.title: Two-step linear equations
forms.skill: Undo addition, then undo multiplication — in that order.

variants.statement: Solve: $2x - 2 = 2$.
variants.explanation: Undo the addition first: $2x = 4$, then divide by $2$: $x = 2$. Check: $2\cdot 2 - 2 = 2$.
Undo the addition first: $2x = 6$, then divide by $2$: $x = 3$. Check: $2\cdot 3 + 8 = 14$.

variants.statement: Solve: $2x - 6 = 2$.
variants.explanation: Undo the addition first: $2x = 8$, then divide by $2$: $x = 4$. Check: $2\cdot 4 - 6 = 2$.
Undo the addition first: $2x = 10$, then divide by $2$: $x = 5$. Check: $2\cdot 5 + 4 = 14$.
Undo the addition first: $2x = -4$, then divide by $2$: $x = -2$. Check: $2\cdot -2 - 2 = -6$.

variants.statement: Solve: $2x + 8 = 2$.
variants.explanation: Undo the addition first: $2x = -6$, then divide by $2$: $x = -3$. Check: $2\cdot -3 + 8 = 2$.

variants.statement: Solve: $2x - 3 = 9$.

Undo the addition first: $3x = 6$, then divide by $3$: $x = 2$. Check: $3\cdot 2 + 8 = 14$.

forms.title: Rational equations
forms.skill: Clear the denominator, solve, then verify the denominator isn't zero.

variants.statement: Solve: $\dfrac{x + 4}{x + 2} = 2$.
variants.explanation: Multiply both sides by $x + 2$ (allowed since $x \ne -2$): $x + 4 = 2(x + 2)$, which gives $x = 0$. The denominator is nonzero there, so it is valid.

variants.statement: Solve: $\dfrac{x + 6}{x + 3} = 2$.
variants.explanation: Multiply both sides by $x + 3$ (allowed since $x \ne -3$): $x + 6 = 2(x + 3)$, which gives $x = 0$. The denominator is nonzero there, so it is valid.

variants.statement: For $f(x) = 2x - 2$, find $f(-2)$.
variants.explanation: Substitute $x = -2$: $f(-2) = -4 - 2 = -6$. Flipping the sign of the constant gives $-2$ — keep the rule exactly as written.
Square first: $(4)^2 = 16$, so $f(4) = 32 - 5 = 27$. Doubling instead of squaring gives $11$ — a square is not a double.

Square first: $(6)^2 = 36$, so $f(6) = 180 + 4 = 184$. Doubling instead of squaring gives $64$ — a square is not a double.

variants.statement: For $f(x) = 5x - 2$, find $f(-3)$.

variants.statement: For $f(x) = 5x + 4$, find $f(5)$.
variants.explanation: Substitute $x = 5$: $f(5) = 25 + 4 = 29$. Flipping the sign of the constant gives $21$ — keep the rule exactly as written.
Square first: $(-4)^2 = 16$, so $f(-4) = 80 - 2 = 78$. Doubling instead of squaring gives $-42$ — a square is not a double.

variants.statement: Find the slope of the line through $(1; -4)$ and $(3; 0)$.

forms.title: Lines through a point
forms.skill: y = mx + b: substitute what you know, solve for what you don't.

variants.statement: A line with slope $2$ passes through $(3; 4)$. Find its $y$-intercept $b$.
variants.explanation: Substitute the point into $y = mx + b$: $b = y - mx = 4 - (6) = -2$. Adding $mx$ instead of subtracting gives $10$.

variants.statement: For which value of $m$ does the system $\begin{cases} x + 2y = 2 \\ 2x + my = 5 \end{cases}$ have NO solution?

variants.statement: Solve by factoring: $x^2 - 7x + 10 = 0$. Find the LARGER root.
variants.explanation: Look for two numbers with product $10$ and sum $7$: that factors as $(x - 5)(x - 2) = 0$, so $x = 5$ or $x = 2$. The larger root is $5$ — picking the smaller one, $2$, is the classic slip.

variants.statement: The parabola $y = (x - 3)^2 - 4$ has its vertex at…
variants.explanation: Vertex form $y = (x - h)^2 + k$ puts the vertex at $(h; k)$: here $(3; -4)$. Watch the sign: $(x - h)$ means the vertex sits at $+h$.

forms.title: Minimum via completing the square
forms.skill: x² + bx + c = (x + b/2)² + (c − b²/4); the constant IS the minimum.

variants.statement: Find the MINIMUM value of $y = x^2 + 4x - 6$.
variants.explanation: Complete the square: $y = (x + 2)^2 + (-10)$. A square is never negative, so the minimum is $-10$, reached at $x = -2$.

forms.title: Parameter for exactly one root
forms.skill: One root ⟺ D = 0; solve for the parameter.

variants.statement: For which $m$ does $x^2 - 4x + m = 0$ have exactly ONE root?
variants.explanation: Exactly one root needs $D = 0$: $(-4)^2 - 4m = 0 \Rightarrow m = \frac{16}{4} = 4$. Then $x^2 - 4x + 4 = (x - 2)^2$ — a perfect square kissing the axis.
